findings.description: Off-by-one in pagination: with a documented 1-based `page`, `offset = page * page_size` makes page=1 return rows 20-39 and the first page of results unreachable; should be `(page - 1) * page_size`.
`name_pattern` is compiled and executed as a regex straight from caller input, enabling ReDoS via catastrophic backtracking patterns like `(a+)+$` against every display_name.
An invalid regex in `name_pattern` (e.g. `[`) raises an uncaught `re.error` that propagates to the caller as an unhandled exception instead of a validation error.

search() loads the entire (or whole-department) users table into memory and filters, sorts, and paginates in Python instead of using SQL ORDER BY/LIMIT/OFFSET, costing O(N) memory and CPU per request.
A negative `page` produces a negative offset and Python slice wraparound, silently returning a window taken from the end of the result list instead of erroring.
`page_size` is clamped only at the upper bound; zero or negative values pass through and yield empty or nonsensical windows instead of being rejected.
If any row has a NULL display_name, the sort key `u["display_name"].lower()` raises AttributeError (and the regex filter on line 60 raises TypeError), crashing the search.
`WHERE email = ?` is case-sensitive under SQLite's default BINARY collation, so by_email misses stored addresses that differ only in case unless input/storage are normalized elsewhere.
If any user row has a NULL department, departments() includes None in its `list[str]` return, violating the declared type contract for callers.
deactivate() returns True even when the user was already deactivated because the UPDATE lacks `AND deleted = 0`, so callers cannot distinguish a real state change from an idempotent no-op.
deactivate() performs a destructive soft-delete with no authorization check or audit logging at this layer, relying entirely on every caller to gate access.
A new sqlite3 connection is opened per method call with default settings (no timeout/WAL tuning), adding per-request overhead and risking 'database is locked' errors under concurrent writes.
Truthiness check `if name_pattern:` is inconsistent with the `department is not None` style used above, though behaviorally harmless since an empty regex matches everything.
Public methods by_email(), departments(), and deactivate() lack docstrings describing semantics, return values, and error behavior, unlike search().
Sorting by `.lower()` is locale/Unicode-naive; `str.casefold()` or a collation-aware key would order international names more correctly.
